caption.text:
May 2019. Eight climbers from four countries set out to scale an unclimbed 6,477-meter peak on the flanks of Nanda Devi East. They never came back.

The team was led by Martin Moran, one of Britain's most respected mountain guides. He had led over 40 exploratory expeditions across the Indian Himalaya since 1992. His team included four Britons, two Americans, an Australian, and an Indian liaison officer. They split into two groups at base camp. Moran's group of eight headed for Peak 6477 — a satellite summit no one had ever climbed.

Their last radio transmission came on May 25. They said they would attempt the summit the next morning. They never returned.

When the other group went to search, they found evidence of a massive avalanche. The trail of footprints ended in a wall of snow and debris. Indian Air Force helicopters spotted five bodies in the avalanche field. A 20-member Indo-Tibetan Border Police team recovered seven bodies after a five-hour operation at 18,000 feet. The eighth was never found.

Manmohan Singh Kohli, the legendary Indian mountaineer who once tried and failed to summit Nanda Devi East, put it bluntly: 'In comparison with Nanda Devi East, Everest is a picnic.'

The mountain had claimed eight lives in a single slide. And the peak they were trying to climb? It remains unclimbed to this day.

Would you risk an unclimbed peak?

text.line_1: Search teams found a massive avalanche.

text.line_2: Would you risk an unclimbed peak?
